data.title: Trump says nuclear deal depends on Saudis normalizing Israel relations
data.description:
President Trump on Thursday said the nuclear agreement between the U.S. and Saudi Arabia is contingent on the Saudis normalizing relations with Israel through the Abraham Accords.

“The Civil Nuclear Deal (There will be no enrichment of material!) being made between the United States Department of Energy and Saudi Arabia, which pertains only to non-military use such as the ones that Iran and UAE (and others) already have, will be approved, but is totally subject to Saudi Arabia joining the very respected and successful Abraham Accords,” Trump wrote on Truth Social.

“The United States is not opposed to Civil (Non-Enriched) Nuclear Facilities,” he added.

Energy Department Secretary Chris Wright and his Saudi counterpart Prince Abdulaziz bin Salman signed the agreement Wednesday on civilian nuclear cooperation, along with a bilateral safeguards agreement. It would be a decades-long deal that would involve U.S. companies and billions of dollars.

Democratic lawmakers and nonproliferation experts have already expressed concern about the deal and what safeguards it contains, along with what this deal could mean for the region amid the Iran war.

The deal and its details have not been released. It will be sent to Congress for review.

Trump has long sought to add countries to his Abraham Accords, which were signed during his first term.

Saudi Arabia for its part has said that normalizing relations with Israel would first need clear progress towards a Palestinian state, something that Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has dismissed.

The Israeli prime minister’s office said Thursday, “The joint American and Israeli military action against the genocidal regime in Teheran and Israel’s crushing of Iran’s terror axis have created the possibility to expand the circle of peace.”

“As President Trump has said, Saudi Arabia’s joining of the Abraham Accords would be an historic leap forward for peace in the Middle East,” the statement said. “Peace through Strength.”

Several countries have already signed the Abraham Accords, including the United Arab Emirates, Bahrain, Sudan and Morocco.

data.title: Homan on how ICE has improved since January
data.description:
White House border czar Tom Homan on Wednesday discussed how Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) has changed since January, when two U.S. citizens were killed in Minneapolis by federal immigration authorities.

“You know, I was honest with the American people, you know, in the last few months that the ICE arrest numbers are down because the department was shut down, right?” Homan said on NewsNation’s “Katie Pavlich Tonight.”

“The Democrats shut the department down mainly because of, you know, what occurred in Minneapolis. I was a part of the negotiation team on the hill. And everything they brought up — they wanted changes before they opened the government — was a reflection on Minnesota,” he added.

Earlier this year, the Trump administration faced backlash for its immigration operations in Minnesota, where Alex Pretti and Renee Good were killed by federal immigration authorities. Pretti and Good’s deaths inflamed already intense anger toward the administration over its immigration agenda.

In May, a record-long Department of Homeland Security (DHS) shutdown came to an end after months of negotiations between congressional Democrats and Republicans.

Democrats had opposed funding for ICE and Border Patrol, both under DHS, amid the Trump administration’s crackdown on immigration and following the deaths of Pretti and Good. In the agreement to end the shutdown, the two agencies did not receive additional funding.

In June, President Trump signed into law a congressional budget package to fund ICE and Border Control for the remainder of his term. The reconciliation funding legislation handed the agencies almost $70 million, which was split between ICE, Border Control and DHS’s discretionary spending budget.

“But, as soon as we got the reconciliation done, we got the funding, numbers are up. I mean, in June, we had the highest numbers of arrests in the history of the agency, of ICE. And July’s tracking to even beat that. So, we’re hitting on all cylinders now. We’re deportation-maxing. Since we’re fully funded, we just got to keep going,” Homan said Wednesday.

However, intense scrutiny toward ICE has returned in recent weeks, with two fatal shootings by the agency in Maine and Texas.

data.title: The Americas' common agenda has a clear voice: Marco Rubio
data.description:
It is no accident that Secretary of State Marco Rubio was among the first to embrace a hemispheric variation on the most famous slogan in American politics: “Make the Americas Great Again.”

What reads at first as wordplay in fact carries considerable weight, because it names something that has rarely been harder to deny. The agendas of the U.S. and much of Latin America are advancing, increasingly and perhaps inevitably, in the same direction, closing gaps that have often widened in the past. 

But this kind of alignment, however, does not happen on its own. It requires an articulator: someone with the political skill to translate shared interests into working policy. It would be simplistic to credit any single author for the alignment now taking shape across the hemisphere. But it is, in my view, unmistakable that Rubio has been central to it. 

In recent history, hardly anyone else at the highest administration level has a better knowledge and understanding of the Western Hemisphere. The son of Cuban immigrants and the first Latino to lead the State Department, he has given the Western Hemisphere the place in American strategic thinking that durable partnership requires: not an afterthought, not a problem file, but a political vision where the United States stands to benefit as much as its continental allies.  

That strategic realignment, led by Rubio, is visible in three developments that deserve close attention.

The first is the administration’s greater use of political ambassadors and, by extension, of embassies as full-fledged instruments of economic diplomacy. Where economic statecraft was once quarantined in specialized bureaus, it now runs through the missions’ chiefs themselves. By the secretary’s own account, American ambassadors’ engagements today concern very specific economic matters, from critical minerals to strategic infrastructure. 

Latin America is living proof of this new model. Missions across the region are fully staffed, and a notable share are led by political ambassadors with a direct line to the White House: Bernie Navarro in Peru, Kevin Marino Cabrera in Panamá and Peter Lamelas in Argentina, among others.

Taken together, they represent an investment that speaks volumes about where the hemisphere now sits in American strategic thinking. Cooperation grounded not in assistance but in the alignment of interests; a distinction the administration has come to grasp, and one Secretary Rubio has proven an effective messenger for.

The second is the substance of the common agenda itself. As the U.S. recalibrates its strategy toward the region, the real work of diplomacy lies in identifying the areas where U.S. and Latin American interests converge and translating those shared interests into practical cooperation — particularly through the implementation of the 2025 National Security Strategy.

In combating transnational crime, countries across the hemisphere increasingly view the disruption of illicit economies and transnational criminal organizations not as a rhetorical objective but as a shared operational priority.

Beyond security cooperation, there is also a common interest in preserving strategic balance in the region. This reflects the administration’s broader objective of shaping common priorities and strengthening hemispheric cooperation to advance regional security and stability, while also reinforcing energy security and resilient supply chains for critical U.S. industries, including defense.

Finally, underpinning all of this is the political momentum that has made such convergence possible. The administration has made no secret of its preference for the bilateral over the multilateral. Its interests are not up for negotiation, so it seeks out, directly, the countries that already share them.

In this sense, Latin America increasingly qualifies, shifting rightward with recent election victories by Keiko Fujimori in Peru, Jose Antonio Kast in Chile and Abelardo de la Espriella in Colombia. An electoral map guarantees nothing, but political affinity lowers friction and removes roadblocks. 

It is this convergence of factors that has enabled the Western Hemisphere. The U.S. will reclaim its position as the principal ally for Latin American governments, who now, more than ever, are willing to share the burden of regional security and stability.

But every repositioning of this magnitude needs someone to connect its pieces and, perhaps most importantly, to lead its purpose — and that role has fallen to Rubio, who has proven equal to it.   

Alfredo Ferrero is Peru’s ambassador to the United States.

data.title: Rubio: Trump military policy for Iran is 'head for an eye'
data.description:
Secretary of State Marco Rubio warned early Thursday that President Trump is prepared to direct an outsized military response to Iranian aggression as the two sides continue to exchange fire nearly five months into the conflict.

Rubio responded to Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi’s statement that Tehran’s policy is an “eye for an eye” in comments to reporters at The Association of Southeast Asian Countries’s 2026 summit in Manila, Philippines. 

“The president’s policy is a head for an eye,” he said. “I mean honestly, that’s what it’s going to be. I mean, they will pay a very heavy price for the things they’re doing.”

Araghchi issued the warning Wednesday in a post on social platform X.

“Our defense doctrine is clear: eye for an eye,” the foreign minister wrote. “Any aggression against Iran, including our infrastructure, will compel a powerful and decisive response.”

These threats follow a brief period of optimism last month that the war could soon reach a resolution after the U.S. and Iran signed a memorandum of understanding that initiated a halt in fighting and renewed peace deal talks. 

However, both Tehran and Washington have alleged that the other side violated the terms of this ceasefire deal and launched attacks in response. 

A rising death toll among U.S. troops and Iranian citizens has increased pressure on the Trump administration to wind down these military operations, with several Republican lawmakers expressing dissatisfaction with the way the president has handled this conflict. 

“There’s got to be an off-ramp, that’s all there is to it,” Sen. Jim Justice (R-W.Va.) told reporters on Wednesday. 

On Wednesday, Trump attended a dignified transfer of four American service members who were recently killed in Jordan and Iraq amid the war.

Rubio told reporters on Wednesday that Tehran is “probably not ready” to make a deal but that its leaders “will be soon.”

“Because the price they’re paying is very high … Despite all that bravado, they are suffering tremendously and they’re going to suffer more until they come to their senses,” he said. “They’re not there yet, unfortunately.”

Alexander Bolton contributed reporting.

data.title: Nearly 1.6 million eggs recalled over salmonella concerns: Full list of products impacted
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) – Nearly 1.6 million white and brown eggs have been recalled over possible contamination, according to a voluntary recall notice shared by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration.

Specifically, the recall notice from Midwest Poultry Services explains the eggs may be contaminated with the bacterium salmonella enteritidis.

Contaminated eggs can appear normal, but when raw or undercooked, they can lead to illness. Officials in Pennsylvania say the bacterium can appear in the eggs if the hen has been infected.

Impacted by this recall are 1,589,577 cartons of white eggs and brown cage-free eggs. They were produced between June 6 and July 3 of this year, and bear sell by or best by dates between July 20, 2026, and August 17, 2026.

The eggs were shipped to multiple locations:

  • Foodservice and retail customers in Texas, Oklahoma, and Louisiana
  • Kroger stores in Texas and Louisiana
  • Brookshire Grocery stores in Texas, Oklahoma, Arkansas, Louisiana, New Mexico, and Mississippi

According to the recall, the eggs were also available at “other smaller retail outlets.”

Have my eggs been recalled?

The table below outlines the exact egg products that have been recalled. No other Midwest Poultry Services eggs are impacted.

The code number can be found on the left or right side of the carton.

The slideshow below shows what the aforementioned recalled cartons look like, and where to find the product and date codes.

There have been no illnesses reported in connection with these recalled eggs. If you have any cartons impacted by the recall, you are encouraged to return them for a full refund, and to not eat the eggs.

What to know about the salmonella infections

Salmonella infections are commonly associated with diarrhea, fever and stomach cramps, according to the CDC. Symptoms can begin between six hours and six days after you’ve ingested the bacteria. Most people are able to recover without receiving treatment within four to seven days.

Illnesses may be more severe for young children, the elderly, and those with weakened immune systems. Some may require medical treatment or hospitalization, the CDC explains.

More severe illness linked to salmonella can include arterial infections, endocarditis (an infection in the heart), and arthritis.

Every year, salmonella causes roughly 1.35 million illnesses and 420 deaths.

data.title: Trump says he'll hold Iran responsible for future Houthi attacks on ships
data.description:
President Trump said Thursday he will “hold Iran responsible” if Yemen’s Houthi rebels launch additional attacks on ships, after the group said its forces struck a pair of Saudi Arabian oil tankers in the Red Sea. 

“A year ago the United States of America attacked, very powerfully, the Houthis, for their interference with commerce and trade, by shooting at ships,” Trump wrote on Truth Social, referring to the U.S. military’s nearly two-month campaign against the Houthis last year. 

American forces struck the Houthis from March to May 2025 in response to the Iranian-backed terrorist group’s attacks on commercial ships in the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden.

On Thursday, the Houthis said they hit two Saudi tankers, named Encelia and Layla, using ballistic and cruise missiles along with drones, according to its SABA news agency. It marked the first known attacks since the militant group announced a blockade of Saudi ships attempting to go through the Bab el-Mandeb Strait on Monday.

Trump said Thursday the Houthis have “acted very responsibly” since the U.S. concluded its military campaign against them in May 2025 and during the war with Iran. 

“Unfortunately, now they are starting up again, shooting at two Saudi Arabian ships last night,” the president added. “Please let this TRUTH serve to represent that if they do this again, the U.S. will hold Iran responsible, in that the Houthis are a Surrogate and/or Proxy of Iran, and major military punishment will be inflicted upon Iran and, of course, the Houthis, themselves, who I am very disappointed with in that they have, until now, acted very professionally and smart.”

The U.S. is already carrying out daily strikes against Iran, completing the 12th consecutive night of attacks on Wednesday. U.S. Central Command said its forces struck Iranian maritime capabilities, missile and drone storage facilities, coastal surveillance sites, and air defense assets.

The Houthis said Thursday its forces “will continue their naval operations” against Saudi Arabia, which has led a coalition — with arms support from the U.S. — against the terror group during the ongoing 11-year Yemeni civil war.

The group imposed the blockade on Saudi ships after it blamed the Middle Eastern nation for a strike against the Sanaa International Airport in Yemen’s northern region, which the Houthis control. The internationally recognized and Saudi-backed Yemeni government in the south claimed responsibility for the strike. 

“We affirm to the Saudi enemy that any folly or aggression targeting our country will be met with large-scale operations deep within its territory, and the consequences will be dire for it, by Allah’s will and power,” the Houthis said Thursday.

Together with the Iranian military’s restrictions on shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, the Houthi crackdown on Saudi shipping through the Bab el-Mandeb Strait threatens to further jolt energy markets.

In 2023, roughly 29.6 million barrels traveled through the passageways combined, accounting for about 28 percent of the world’s oil consumption that year, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration.

Oil prices spiked Thursday after the Houthis said they attacked the two ships. The price of Brent crude oil, the international benchmark, is up 5.5 percent to $99.25.

data.title: What's the bigger problem for today's Democrats — senility or socialism?
data.description:
The democratic socialists are on a roll, defeating longtime incumbents in primaries and running New York City. Every political commentator is hyperventilating over the socialist takeover of the Democratic Party. 

But there is another thread common among the Democratic primary losers: They’re old. 

Of the five Democratic U.S. House incumbents who lost, three races featured long-tenured incumbents with an average age of 72 and an average age gap against the winners of 40 years. Those three high-profile incumbents, Al Green (D-Texas), Adriano Espaillat (D-N.Y.) and Diana DeGette (D-Colo.), were reliable progressives — not full-on socialists, but hardly moderates. 

The GovTrack ideology rankings for the full U.S. Congress from 2024 put Espaillat as the 19th most liberal House member — two spots ahead of socialist hero Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-N.Y.). Green ranks 61st, better than Reps. Ro Khanna and Kweisi Mfume. DeGette was the “worst” as the 105th most liberal, roughly on par with then-Rep. (now Sen.) Lisa Blunt Rochester (D-Del.) and Rosa DeLauro (D-Conn.).

Maybe they are not liberal enough, but the bigger difference looks to be age rather than ideology. Green, the first casualty, was also re-districted together with Rep. Christian Menefee. Menefee, a 38-year-old, first-term member, was endorsed by the Progressive Change Committee. Green had the overwhelming support of his colleagues, but he had been in public office for nearly 20 years.

In New York, Espaillat, chair of the Congressional Hispanic Caucus, lost to 32-year-old first-time candidate Darializa Avila Chevalier. Being a Top 20 liberal was not enough for the 71-year-old congressman who had been in public office for nearly 30 years. 

DeGette, of Colorado, is the youngster of the group, at a mere 68 years old. She lost to 29-year-old Melat Kiros, who has never held public office. As a Ph.D. student with a brief stint as a practicing lawyer, she may have the thinnest resume of any incoming member of Congress.  

But none of that mattered. 

This youthquake stretches back to last year’s New York City mayoral race, when then 34-year-old Zohran Mamdani defeated 68-year-old Andrew Cuomo. In and out of government since 1982, Cuomo was all the things younger voters despise in one smarmy package. He had been on the statewide ballot four times since 2006. Hounded out of office by credible sexual harassment accusations, which he lamely brushed off as “playful” and “misinterpreted,” he proceeded to hook up for a nice paycheck from an overseas crypto exchange. It would be hard to construct a candidate with such high name recognition and heavy baggage.

Although both parties have a problem with old people clinging to power, it is a worse problem for Democrats, whose voters skew much younger than Republicans’ voters. According to Pew Research, voters 18-29 identify as Democrat at 54 percent versus 38 percent Republican. The gap in the 30-49 cohort is narrower, at 49 percent Democrat versus 41 percent Republican. In the Democratic Party, youth must be served.

Compounding the problem is recent history. While the public could rightly question the continued tenure of 92-year-old Sen. Chuck Grassley (R-Iowa) and 84-year-old Mitch McConnell (R-Ky.), neither has had quite the meltdown of the late Sen. Dianne Feinstein (D-Calif.) went through in her last years.

But Joe Biden may have been the one to push younger voters over the edge. Despite clear signs of decline and a catastrophic debate performance, Biden, then 81, refused to step down. Only when faced with overwhelming polling evidence and a full-scale revolt, which included 84-year-old Nancy Pelosi, did Biden relent.

Unfit to run for president but apparently fit enough to be president, Biden loomed over the Harris campaign with his terrible polling numbers and inability to implement any policy changes to help her.

My guess is the shock of President Trump’s victory (and it should not have been a shock) has impelled the younger cohorts of Democratic voters to take out their fury at the old Democratic Party establishment. They were not going to pin the blame on the Black female nominee, that’s for sure. And both Biden and Barack Obama added a fuel to the far-left’s anger with their friendly post-election interactions with Trump.

Biden continues to haunt the Democratic Party, like a wheezing, doddering ghost. He and his loyalists still insist he would have beaten Trump — which is ridiculous. And now he has a book coming out with undoubtedly an excruciating tour to follow — meaning it’s only going to get worse for the old guard. 

It is great fun to debate socialism, and it sure gets clicks. But the Democratic establishment would be well served to ditch its sclerotic gerontocracy. 

Keith Naughton, a longtime Republican political consultant, is co-founder of Silent Majority Strategies, a public and regulatory affairs consulting firm, and a former Republican political campaign consultant in Pennsylvania.

data.title: Live updates: Senate panel takes up Blanche nomination; Congress votes on separate war powers resolutions
data.description:
The Senate Judiciary Committee is scheduled to vote Thursday morning on the nomination of acting Attorney General Todd Blanche to take the role permanently. Republican Sens. John Cornyn (Texas) and Thom Tillis (N.C.) have not committed to backing him, and their opposition could sink the nomination.

And Dr. Erica Schwartz faces a vote in the Senate Health, Education, Labor and Pensions (HELP) Committee for her nomination to be director of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

The House convenes Thursday morning to vote on a Democrat-sponsored war powers resolution. The Senate will vote on a separate but similiar resolution. They come as President Trump’s support for the Iran conflict is reaching a new low among his base.

Trump is also threatening to intensify the war with Iran as some Republican lawmakers urge him to “finish the job,” but there is also widespread skepticism on Capitol Hill on both sides of the aisle about whether he can deliver a victory.

The Energy Department announced Wednesday a nuclear cooperation deal with Saudi Arabia, and it drew swift backlash. Insiders can read more here.

White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt will hold a press briefing today at 11 a.m. EDT. Following, Trump is set to visit the Environmental Protection Agency and will make a statement about the Ratepayer Protection Pledge, a pledge he issued in March in which U.S. artificial intelligence (AI) companies cover the electricity costs for their data centers, not consumers.

Follow live below.

data.title: Fatal police shooting in Wisconsin sparks investigation, protest
data.description:
The fatal shooting of a man by police officers in Madison, Wis., on Wednesday is sparking protests.

In a statement, the Madison Police Department (MPD) said the man injured an officer with a fix-blade knife while he was resisting arrest. After officers unsuccessfully tried to get the man into custody using a taser, the injured officer shot the man, according to authorities.

MPD also noted its officers provided “life-saving measures” to the man, whose identity has not been revealed, on the scene. The victim, in his thirties, was transported to the hospital and later pronounced dead.

“I want to take a moment to honestly and sincerely offer my heartfelt sympathies to his family for this loss,” Madison Police Chief John Patterson told reporters Wednesday, referring to the deceased man.

A video of the incident shared on Instagram shows one officer kicking the man down, while another officer approaches and shoots him while he’s on the ground, according to Nexstar affiliate WFRV-TV in Green Bay, Wisc. 

The MPD acknowledged the cell phone footage but said it is “only one perspective” of the incident. The authorities are also reviewing additional video.

The shooting occurred in the Marquette neighborhood, after officers were dispatched there for a person allegedly “checking parked vehicles,” Patterson said. 

“Officers located the man, they attempted to contact him, but he fled on a bicycle and then traveled through some nearby backyards, we think [in] an attempt to get away,” he added.

After reconnecting with the man, officers engaged in a physical struggle with the man in an intersection — during which the officer who shot the man was injured. Another officer was injured in the incident, but Patterson said it is “not clear how that occurred” and he “does not believe” those injuries were from the man’s knife.  

The Wisconsin Department of Justice Division of Criminal Investigation is probing the incident. The MPD also said it is placing all four officers involved on administrative leave. 

A crowd formed near where the shooting occurred on Wednesday, as people chanted, “Arrest the cops!” according to The Associated Press. Protesters also marched to the Wisconsin State Capitol and took the stage at a summer concert series, which the Wisconsin Chamber Orchestra later canceled, the AP reported.

Wisconsin Gov. Tony Evers (D) said Wednesday he was briefed on the shooting and will “continue to receive updates” as information is released. 

“There must be transparency and accountability in any investigation moving forward,” Evers wrote on social platform X.

“We will continue to keep the loved ones and family of the individual who was killed in our hearts and prayers. His life mattered,” the governor added later in a separate post. “As folks exercise their First Amendment rights tonight and in the days ahead, we urge them to do so peacefully and respectfully.”

data.title: To end the wars in Ukraine and Iran, starve Russia's gas pumps
data.description:
Sanctioning Russia’s gasoline imports could lead to peace in Ukraine — and Iran.  

Fistfights at Russian gas stations are a sign that Vladimir Putin’s war is finally coming home, giving President Trump an opportunity to end the conflict. Ukrainian drone strikes are destroying Russian energy infrastructure at an impressive pace, producing painful gasoline shortages.

President Trump should use this opportunity wisely and achieve peace through strength, as the late Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.) cwould have argued.

If Trump can continue assisting Ukrainian targeting of Russian energy infrastructure while denying Moscow both gasoline imports and revenue streams, he may be able to force Putin to the negotiating table and secure peace. If peace is secured, Ukraine could then repurpose its vast drone and counter-drone production to support the coalition against Iran. Defeating Russia may therefore secure peace in both Ukraine and the Middle East.

Ukraine’s drone strikes may be turning the tide in the four-year conflict. Ukraine’s aerial and seaborne drones have struck at least 35 vessels over 96 hours from July 5 to 9, knocked out more than half of Russia’s oil refining capacity, driven refinery run rates to their lowest level in 21 years and even struck an oil terminal and naval base in Saint Petersburg ahead of an economic forum attended by Putin.  

Russian popular and elite opinion is souring on the war, and gasoline is a primary reason. Due to Putin’s war of choice, Russia has suffered an astonishing 1.4 million battlefield casualties, with perhaps 450,000 deaths. Despite these grim figures, many Russians have been insulated from the conflict, as Russian casualties have been overwhelmingly concentrated in poorer soldiers from the portions of Russia outside of Moscow and St. Petersburg, the country’s two largest and most politically sensitive cities. 

For the first time, gasoline outages are forcing many ordinary Russians to confront the profound failure of Putin’s war. Moscow has banned exports of jet fuel, gasoline and now, diesel, while gas stations across Russia are starting to see hours-long queues and even fistfights.  

Facing domestic gasoline shortages, Russia is turning to its partners for more imports. Belarus, Russia’s partner in the Union State agreement, has ramped up gasoline exports. China is a major wildcard. Since the start of the U.S.-Iran conflict, it has restricted exports of refined products but recently reversed this decision. Kazakhstan, facing fuel shortages of its own, is restricting exports.  

India is a crucial actor and should be the focus of U.S. diplomatic efforts. India was historically close with the Soviet Union, and Russia supplies India’s refineries with crude oil and its military with the S-400 air defense system. With Russia facing fuel shortages, Moscow has looked to Indian refineries, including the Vadinar refinery, partially owned by Russian oil giant Rosneft, to supply it with gasoline.

The Trump administration should use a mixture of carrots and sticks to deter Indian gasoline sales to Russia. India is arguably the United States’ closest partner in technology, and the two sides share enduring interests. Diplomacy — including the easing of tariffs on Indian exports — could persuade New Delhi to cease gasoline shipments to Russia. Sticks may prove necessary for most other actors. Washington, working in concert with Brussels and other allied capitals, should warn that gasoline sales to Russia will provoke financial sanctions.

Additionally, if Ukraine’s drone complex were freer to export — after domestic rearmament — U.S. global security interests would benefit. Kyiv is the free world’s arsenal to produce cheap, attritable drone strike and counter-drone systems. Against Iran, attritable drones could degrade Tehran’s forces without expending scarce, exquisite munitions, while shared counter-drone systems deployed across the Gulf would blunt the Shahed drone threat and free ballistic-missile interceptors for other fronts.

Finally, victory in Ukraine, and enabling its drones for exports, would deter adversaries elsewhere. Beijing is surely noting Ukraine’s successful drone strike campaign against Russia’s shadow fleet, and pondering whether variants launched from Taiwan could render a quarantine, blockade or invasion intolerable. If Russia is defeated, Ukraine’s drone capabilities would help the free world deter other adversaries, from the Persian Gulf to the Taiwan Strait.  

President Trump has an opportunity to quickly resolve up to two wars and potentially prevent a third one. If he can prevent Russia from obtaining gasoline imports, and thereby ramp up pressure on Russian consumers, he may be able to bring Putin to the negotiating table. In so doing, Trump would provide a coda to Graham’s legacy: peace in Ukraine, and perhaps beyond.

Joseph Webster is a senior fellow at the Atlantic Council and editor of the independent China-Russia Report.
